speaker: interviewer
text: We've been talking with Helen Zelinger in Northbrook, Illinois. This is tape two. You had started to talk about your leaving home. And you never saw your home again. Continue that story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry. [INAUDIBLE]. Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two with Helen Zelinger, June 9, 1995 in Northbrook. You were talking about your escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We came to Lublin. And I don't remember the name, but we stayed with some very lovely Jewish rich people. They had a big business. And they had a humongous home. So they took us in for the night. The next morning, we had to leave. At the time, my father told me that there is a pact between Russia and Germany that the Germans would not attack the Russians. So my father's philosophy was we're going to run as far as we can till we get to the Russian border. And there, we will stay and wait what's going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, the government man that you met--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, we met in Lublin, at the same people. He was not really the prime minister. I don't know if he was charge of the army or whatever. And we spoke a beautiful Polish. My father spoke a great Polish, too. So he spoke with them, because the people gave us a dinner together. But we did not know who those people were. He had his own chauffeur. He had a beautiful taxi.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember sitting at the table, that my father ask him, because being in the government, maybe he knew-- not maybe, but for sure he knew more than we did know at the time. My father gave him a question, how far would the Polish army let the German army come in? So he gave him an example. He said, if you take a pillow that has down in it and you put your hand in with a fist, at the beginning, your fist goes in very fast. But at far-- as further you go, then it stop of the resistance. So he said, see. The Germans will go as far as to the big river Vistula. And then we will stop them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was a good attitude towards us. We felt, you know, maybe that's going to happen. The next morning, we did not see him, because what the owners told us, that he was going towards Romania, escaping from Poland himself. So we just continued our journey. Had that episode. But that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find gas in Lublin?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did not find gas in Lublin. We drove on--

speaker: interviewer
text: Ethanol?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ethanol. And that's-- wherever we came in Poland, ethanol was used a lot. Some people didn't have any, any other source of heating, so they used to have, like, two burners in the house. And they used to burn this on ethanol. So my father got a supply. How or what, I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Because you said that you left without money, without jewels.

speaker: interviewee
text: Without money, without jewelry. The only fortune thing that we had that a lot of people knew my father. At the time, they trusted him. I really don't know. I have no answer for this, how we did. There was no money with us. And that's the way we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, the people from Lublin weren't escaping? They--

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know what happened to them later, because we were there very early in the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from Lublin, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Lublin, as I say, we went as far-- oh, we went-- I think the next big city was Lutsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-U-C-K. And this was already a little on the Ukrainian side. Over there, it's again, I think the Jews were very courteous to us. They gave us, four, four of us, they gave us one little home in their apartment. And we felt very comfortable under the circumstances.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these people you knew from before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Strange, completely strange people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's just knocking from one door to the next one. And who was nice opened a door, and who was mean did not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many people fleeing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. It's a matter of fact that by the city of the Jewish community in Lublin was even set up a dorm where they cooked meals. Because where would you eat? A lot of people. And according to stories from my husband-- he was in Lublin, too, which I never met him there. And he used to go to same place to eat for the food that I with my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then one sunny day, the Russians were in already. I missed the whole story. I missed the whole story, the way the Russians took away our truck. We went to the border, to the Russian border.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a-- in a truck or in a car?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we had-- it's-- yeah, it's like a car, OK. But at the borderline, staying there a week, the Russians came in. The Russians took away everything from us, what we had, and put us on their military trucks. And with them, we went into Lutsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot that episode. And in one nice day, walking on the street, they stopped my father. And they ask to identify himself. And he showed his documents. And they took him in and want him to become a Russian citizen. And he refused.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then they asked where the family is. And he brought them home. We all were home. And they took us, all four of us.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us. We refused to become Russian citizens. They took us. They said, bundle up, whatever you have. And they took us to freight trains to ship us out to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Lutsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Lutsk..

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what were you doing for clothes at this time, because you only had your uniform and--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were helps. As a child, I don't remember those details, if the parents bought it, if there were organizations that they had pity on you and gave you some clothes. But my father worked, also, on the side, whoever needed him for something, for the few dollars. I-- to be honest, I cannot answer that question.

speaker: interviewer
text: One thing I didn't ask you was the name of his brewery.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, there was not really a name. There was Rynski. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, all the, the bills and everything is on Mayer Rynski, but no special name.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. What were your feelings during this time? Were you frightened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You didn't have your home. You didn't know what tomorrow brings. You didn't know where the meal the next day comes. There was only hope that a miracle was going to happen, that peace will come. I do remember a lot of people then yet, before the Russians took us to the freight trains, used to come in to the borders, escape from the Germans. One gentleman met my father, and he was also from Szczekociny. I don't know the name. But he told him that his father was still alive. And he begged him to come back, because the business are so great that he's missing all the things. But we had no desire. We had an opportunity to go back if we wanted to. There was a German commission. The Jews went to them and told your problems, why you want to go back or whatever. That was the German occupation, already. And there were camps already. A lot of people went. And they went back. And they all are dead. But my father said, no, we won't do that. So maybe this is our luck that we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year was it when you went to the freight train?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- September-- late, late, late '39, by the end of '39, maybe beginning of '40.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was winter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a tough winter or a mild winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a tough winter. We'd-- like you say, there were about maybe 40, 50 people in one-- in one freight. And there were no comforts. You had to sleep sitting up. I remember very well that my father got very ill. And they had a special [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one-- wagon where was the Blue Cross, the Russian Blue Cross-- Red Cross, excuse me. And they took him out to give him some help. And we thought, we will never see him again. And I remember the trauma, crying and pleading of not taking him. But they were good enough. And after a few days, we saw our dad again, which was a great feeling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you warm enough? Did you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were not warm. We had lice. It was dirty. They used to stop on stations going for hundreds and hundreds of miles. And if it came to a station, the only thing which we got is a little water. And basically, they gave us boiled water, which it used to be called in Russian, [RUSSIAN]. That means boiled water. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And finally, we came after-- I don't remember, it was a month or six week-- to the destination what they planned for us. And that was Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were all the people in the freight Jewish people escaping?

speaker: interviewee
text: In ours, basically. But I believe they took anybody who refuse to become Russian citizen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the people in the train arrange things?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't pleasant, if I can tell you a little episode.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew that my grandma from Sosnowiec and grandpa and my Aunt Sarah were in one of those trains, also. So on each stop, where they stop, I used to go out with my brother and just yell loudly, [NON-ENGLISH]. But there was no response. And one lovely day, [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] we went out. And there was a response. So we found my grandma, grandpa, and Sarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we wanted to take them into our car. And the people were against it, because it was very crowded. And they wondered if we should come to theirs. And they wouldn't allow us, either. So that was the last time I saw my grandparents. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: They took him to [? Takistan, ?] which we found out this after the liberation from concentration camp. My Uncle Mendel, who was in Australia, found out-- I don't know how-- that we are alive in Russia. So to him, we got the telegram that Sarah is alive. And she is in Russia, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they separated the trains, and you went to Siberia and they went to Tashkent-- [? Takistan? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Takistan. ?] And they died there. They got sick of cholera. They used to sleep in parks. There was no place for them. Nights, they were in the parks. And when my grandma-- first, my grandpa died. This I know from the-- my aunt's stories, that my grandma died. But when she was hospitalized, my aunt pretended that she is also sick. So she was with her in the hospital, next bed, being sick. But when Grandma died, she had no choice. Buried her. And she was going on living, working for the Russians till her departure to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1945, the end of 1945. It's again, we were in Cracow, in Poland, also. And we heard that people-- every day comes a different shipment of people from Russia. So it wasn't me, because I was in school. It was my brother, who my mom used to send every day to this station. And he used to call, Sarah, Sarah, Sarah. And one day, Sarah was there. So that was a reunion with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then did she stay with you after?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She stayed with us till she got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, the train opened. It was the end of the stop. And you were somewhere. Did you know where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They told us. The, the [INAUDIBLE], or whatever it's called in Russia, they told us that we going to Siberia. They dropped us off, actually, in a town, which I don't remember. But then there was sleds that they brought us in in a small village called [? Yog. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russian, would be Yog, Y-- Y-O-G. I mean, not in Russian, in yeah-- and over there, when they let us out, the only thing what we saw is very few little homes. You don't call them homes, tents, and forest and snow. And there was no clothes. You couldn't walk in a pair of shoes in that snow. The snow almost went to your umbilical cord. And they put us in-- gave us one room. There were no beds. There were no tables. There was nothing in there. Somehow, little by little, they brought in a wooden table. They brought in one chair. They make a bunk bed that we slept on it. And the windows were closed up with plywood because there was no glass. I do remember as a child, I was always hungry. And I always cold. Every morning at 7 o'clock, my father would go to that special store in that little town where they would give you the ration of the bread. And he brought home that piece of bread. You almost could make clay from it or make some toys. That's the way it looked, black. It almost looked like mortar than it was a piece of bread. There were towns surrounded. So they told you if you want to eat, you have to go in back by the farmers, which my father did quite a few times. And it wasn't pleasant. We were-- we were hungry day and night. There wasn't anything to eat. But the main thing which my father decide, that he will never go to work. In the First World War, he lost one eye, which he was blind on one.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: He tells me a lot of stories. But I really don't remember them, if he was-- he wasn't shot, but he was hurt something. He got the infection.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was, in the First World War. Yes. And the infection wasn't attended. So he lost the vision. My mother at the time was very young, also. So they decided they not going to work. They have to get out from that little village or else we all going to die. It used to come once a month to the village, a Russian doctor, a woman, and have the complaints from the people, examine who's to see, who was well and who was sick. Well, my mother had always a complaint that she's bleeding, which they really did not know that. They didn't ask to show or didn't examine. My father, again, which legitimately said he's blind. It took a few months. And they decided they're going to ship us out from there, which it was a miracle. They decide that Mom and Dad belong to an invalid home, where they going to be taken care. I will never forget that day. Was early morning, came a sled with one horse, with a lot of blankets, because it was very cold. It was in the winter. They put us on the sled, covered it up nice and comfortable and warm. And it was like we going to a funeral. The whole village was walking behind us and praying and begging whatever we can do, where we are, to help them. And I remember the departure with the sled. But then we had to go on the water, also. Which it was a small ship. And they brought us to another little town called Kupiansk. That was the home of the invalid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-U-P-I-C-K. My parents were given a lovely room. But I had a brother at that time, that he was four years younger than I. And I was older. They did not allow children to be there. So was a question what to do. With me, they had no choice. And to keep with them, there was no excuse. I'm sick or I'm well. With my brother, they finagled. They told the commission that he is a lunatic, that at night, he walks out from the room. And he walks an hour or two, and then he comes back. The people never checked on it. They believed what they said. And my brother could stay with the parents. I had to go to an orphanage, which it was maybe five miles away from my parents. It was a trauma, because, basically, I was surrounded with children who didn't have parents or their parents left them. But the care was lovely. We all slept-- basically, the same-aged girls. They didn't mix girls and boys in the same big room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think you wouldn't see your parents anymore?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't think that way, because every Sunday I had the privilege to go and visit them. And when I used to come to them, I had a potato to take with, which they saved up. I had a cookie. I had a piece of sugar, whatever they could save up for themself for me. They saved this for me. But when I came back, I couldn't eat myself. So if there is a potato, because there were no potatoes available for us kids, I had to split with all the kids. And they laughed. They looked for Sunday, forward, for me to go and come back with one or two cookies, so we each one can have a little triangle from it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many kids were in your group?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say maybe 20 slept in the same room.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time, was 13 years old. They were clean. I have to admit one thing. The laundry kept us supplied with-- we all wear the same clothes. But we changed our clothes at least three times a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: We wear, basically a-- in the winter, we had those [INAUDIBLE], the high-- the high boots, or else you couldn't walk in them, and coats, warm coats. And in the summer, we wore a skirt and a blouse. But the skirts and the colors used to change off. So they knew what grade is wearing what color. We had to keep spotless, that place. We took turn of washing that big floor, which I had no idea how to do, but it gave me a great school. It gave me discipline. Once a week, we had to clean the toilets. We had to clean the-- wash the floor. Besides this, we had things to do over there. They had their own farms. And after schooling, we had to work on those farms, which it meant everything from picking potatoes to attending to cattle in the morning. Whatever a farm is needed, the kids were doing it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was a totally different life than you had had before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Comparing at home? Doesn't even compare. I mean, it's-- I tell you one episode, also. When I started school there, I went to school. I started fifth grade. I used to sit almost for a year, during the recess, with my hands on the desk. And all the kids from the whole school-- because I was the only one in that town from Europe. And they used to call me [RUSSIAN], white hand. Because none of them had hands like me. The children start working there very young.

speaker: interviewer
text: So every recess, I didn't have a chance to have a bite. I had to sit. They loved me in that school, I have to admit. But I had to sit quietly with my hands on the desk. And they all are, [RUSSIAN]. Means pretty. So it was something like I came down from the moon for them. Because at home you had had maids to do this stuff?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not necessarily maids, but I don't think our parents gave us-- my mother had one maid, a cook. But I don't think they gave us the opportunity to do the things. I remember I used to love to watch when she baked. And that's the way I learned, just from watching, to be a good baker. Because Mom would never let me do it. It was the cleaning up for her or whatever. But the maid would-- I would just sit and observe. And that's the way I learned. And now it helps with a cookbook. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So the hands were always nicely, not chapped. You didn't have to do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you the only Jewish child in this school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, as far as European. I-- you know, they really don't ask if you're Jewish or not. The director-- I have to tell you a little story, also-- was a Jewish lady, which we did not know. When my dad and mom came in to sign me up to the orphanage, assume-- I mean, there was trauma on them. They didn't realize what it is. They didn't know if I will see them. She offered-- she-- I don't know what-- I cannot judge the age, as a youngster. But she wanted to adopt me, because she didn't have children. I even don't know if she was married. And my parents would not sign a paper. But she was very nice to me. She will always sometimes if she will see me that the other kids won't see, she will give me something also to eat or extra. We didn't have in the orphanage a lot to eat. It's the same thing. They give us one piece of bread a day. If it was lunch time, we came into the dorm. And basically, there were potato peels in the water. There were no potatoes. The kids at night, the boys, through the windows, ran to the farm to dig a potato and make, usually, little fire so they can cook the potato. So for the kids, we have comfort that we kept warm. They gave us warm clothes. Food was not available. This was Stalin's time. We used to sing the songs to Stalin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wish you would ask me before. I could-- [RUSSIAN]. I remember a lot of Russian songs. But I wouldn't sing. My voice is not the greatest. Have to tune it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: You could hum a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not really. And I myself-- at that age, I and three or four other girls, I don't remember, we were assigned at dawn, which is like 4:00, 4:30 in the morning, to go on the pasture. And we were responsible, like, for 15 or 17 young calves. That was created a problem. We were young, and we were sleepy. But if something would be missed, we will be blamed. And it's a big penalty for that. We knew that the calves need salt. But Russia did not have salt to put them on. Usually they will all come together and just lick on the salt. So if I may express myself and say so, we used to drink a lot the water. And we took turns to urinate. And this is the way we kept the calves together. So we used to wake up each other. It would take half an hour. But in the morning, taking the calves back, we were delighted. We counted for each of them. So this was basic for quite a few years for me. The summer time off school, we used to go to the forest. In the forest, we used to cut the grass. And we had a certain amount of day to do it. And it wasn't easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you use to cut it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used-- I wish-- I really don't know how to say in English. That [INAUDIBLE]. Is it a [INAUDIBLE]? A big-- with-- uh huh. What is that?

speaker: interviewer
text: A scythe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. It was larger than I was at the time. But they did a good job. You learn to do those things. And you really wanted to finish what they tell you, too. Because they call you [RUSSIAN]. Means you accomplished something. So I didn't want to be different than other children. So I did just as well as all the Russian kids did at the time. There was one day this. And the other day was an assignment, a very large bucket to go and fetch berries. Those-- they made preservatives. And we had something for the winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all in all, it was a not unhappy time for you there.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a not unhappy time because I made good friends. I was young. I had a school, which I enjoyed it. I had-- the school wasn't easy. I have to tell you that going to school every day, four miles, I used skis. Because there was no other way in the snow to go to school.
